claim: Multi-agent systems are distributed systems problems: agents run in separate remote environments communicating through networks, not sub-agents in same process
actionable: When designing multi-agent systems, treat agent communication as distributed systems engineering with message ordering, persistence, and failure recovery requirements
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Future of AI-to-AI communication will use natural language as API instead of JSON schemas, allowing flexible capability negotiation between agents
actionable: Design agent interfaces to accept natural language descriptions of capabilities rather than hard-coded JSON schemas to enable flexible agent-to-agent negotiation
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Message ordering in distributed agent systems is critical: if messages get reordered, LLM performance degrades significantly due to context sensitivity
actionable: Implement strict message ordering guarantees in multi-agent systems; use message queues with ordering guarantees, not eventual consistency messaging
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Real-time delivery is essential for agent communication: polling and async message delivery don't work for agent reasoning loops
actionable: Use push-based communication (websockets) instead of polling for agent-to-agent messaging to support real-time reasoning loop continuity
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Agent session/thread IDs differ by framework (LangGraph uses thread_id, some use conversation_id, run_id, execution_id) and require normalization in multi-agent systems
actionable: When integrating multiple agent frameworks, build a mapping layer that normalizes session IDs across frameworks to maintain consistent context
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Agents need persistent message queues with hydration/rehydration: if agent crashes, it needs to reload messages already processed and context state
actionable: Implement persistent message storage in multi-agent systems with agent rehydration logic to restore context when agents restart
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy

claim: Multi-agent systems need governance layer: audit trails, identity assignment, permissions scoping, multi-tenancy support, and observability for enterprise deployment
actionable: Build enterprise multi-agent systems with audit logging, identity/permission mapping, tenant isolation, and complete message/tool-call recording
source_title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Vlad Luzin: Herding Cats—The Hidden Challenges of Multi-Agent Autonomy
